pages.title: Screen Reader Access
pages.text: Screen Reader Access Hello, world! This is a toast message. Screen Reader Access Accessibility Help Use the accessibility options provided by this Web site to control the screen display. These options allow increasing the text spacing, changing the text size and color scheme for clear visibility and better readability. Changing the Text Size Changing the size of the text refers to making the text appearing smaller or bigger from its standard size.The website allows you to change the text size , by clicking on the text size icons present at the top of each page. Text size Icons Following different options are provided in the form of icons which are available on the top of each page: Decrease text size: Allows to decrease the text size up to one level Normal text size: Allows to set default text size Increase text size: Allows to increase the text size up to one level Changing the Color Scheme Changing the color scheme refers to applying a suitable background and text color that ensures clear readability. Information related to the various screen readers The National Website of India fully complies with Guidelines for Indian Government Websites. Our visitors with visual impairments can access the Portal using Assistive Technologies, such as screen readers. The information of the Website is accessible with different screen readers, such as JAWS, NVDA, SAFA, Supernova and Window-Eyes. Following table lists the information about different screen readers: File View Close

pages.title: Brief History
pages.text: Brief History Hello, world! This is a toast message. Brief History The Development and Welfare Board for the Denotified, Nomadic, and Semi-Nomadic Communities (DWBDNC) a Society Registered under Societies Registration ACT 1860, has been constituted by the Government of India vide Gazette Notification No. 16014/04/2018-BC-III dt. 21.02.2019. 1. The composition of the Board (DWBDNCs) is as below:- (i) Chairperson, to be appointed by the Government of India; (ii) Chief Executive Officer of the Board in the rank of Joint Secretary to the Government of India – Member Secretary; (iii) Joint Secretary, Department of Social Justice & Empowerment dealing with the subject – Member (iv) Representative of Ministry of Tribal Affairs – Member (v) Representative of Department of School Education – Member; (vi) Five eminent persons from or working in the field of denotified, nomadic and semi-nomadic communities to be nominated as Members by the Government of India. 2. The appointment of Chairman and Chief Executive Officer will be made for a period of three years extendable up to five years; 3. The Head Quarter of the Department of Welfare Board for the Denotified, Nomadic and Semi-Nomadic Communities at New Delhi; 4. The terms of reference of the Development and Welfare Board for the Denotified, Nomadic and Semi-Nomadic Communities are as under: a). To formulate and implement Welfare and Development programmes, as required, for Denotified, Nomadic and Semi-Nomadic Communities. b). To identify the locations/areas where these communities are densely populated. c). To assess and identify gaps in accessing existing programmes and entitlements and to collaborate with Ministries/implementing agencies to ensure the ongoing programmes meet the special requirements of Denotified, Nomadic and Semi-Nomadic Communities. d). To monitor and evaluate the progress of the schemes of Government of India and the States/UTs with reference to Denotified, Nomadic and Semi-Nomadic Communities. e). Any other related work as may be assigned by the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment. File View Close

pages.title: Sector Overview
pages.text: Sector Overview Hello, world! This is a toast message. Sector Overview The term ‘De-notified Tribes’ stands for all those communities that were once notified under the Criminal Tribes Acts, enforced by the British Raj between 1871 and 1947. These Acts were repealed by the Indian Government post-Independence. The term nomad is applied to social groups who undertake a fairly frequent, usually seasonal physical movement as part of their livelihood strategy. The term semi-nomad is mostly used to describe those sections of nomads whose duration, distance, and frequency of movement are comparatively less than others. The distinction between nomads and semi-nomads do not invoice distinguishable ethnic categories or social groups, it rather describes the degree of mobility practiced by them. The De-notified, Nomadic and Semi-Nomadic Tribes (DNTs) are among the most economically and socially deprived communities in the country. The constitution of the DWBDNCs by the Government is among one of the most significant step taken in recent years to facilitate socio-economic empowerment of DNTs. The Government has also approved a Central Sector Scheme (CSS) - Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNTs (SEED) and tasked the Board with implementation of the same. File View Close

pages.title: Central Scheme : Livelihood
pages.text: Central Scheme : Livelihood Hello, world! This is a toast message. Central Scheme - Livelihood Seed - Livelihood Eligibility: All DNTs staying in villages and rural areas in India The SEED Livelihoods scheme is provided through the NRLM/NULM Mission under the Ministry of Rural Development. To avail the benefit of this scheme click this link: Clik Here Guidelines: SEED Scheme Guidlines Further Information : Read more PM-DAKSH Beneficiaries: DNTs,SCs,OBCs,EBCs,Transgendered persons of ages 18-45 Eligibility:Age between 18-45 years, No Income limit for SC/DNT/Safai Mitras Benefits: 1) Short Term Training for Jobs: Upto 30 Weeks of Training for jobs as carpenters, tailors, beautician workers, leather work etc. Stipend of Rs. 1000 month 2) Small Business Development 3) Skill Development Required Documents 1. Self-Declaration of Caste 2. Date of Birth 3. Endorsement by Local Pradhan of the Community (Optionally : Aadhar Card and Aadhar Linked Bank Account) Application Process 1) Register on Skill India Digital Hub (SIDH) 2)Profile Verification through Mobile based OTP 3) Selection of Course Important Timelines: All throughout the year. Period of training depends on course. Important Links: https://pmdaksh.dosje.gov.in/ Guidelines: Scheme Guidelines Assistance for Skill Development of OBCs/DNTs/EBCs Beneficiaries: NGOs seeking grants from NBCFDC Eligibility: For NGOs already Funded- a) NGOs which were funded by this scheme earlier than 2014-15 b) NGOs willing to locate the project in a Sansad Adarsh Gram Yojana Village For New NGOs: Application must be through NBCFDC Other Criteria: 90% of the cost of an approved project shall be covered for new projects. 75% of the cost will be covered for NGOs already being provided with funding in the past. List of approved projects here. Required Documents 1. PAN Card of Organisation 2. PAN and Aadhar Card of Office Bearers Application Process Application to be sent to NBCFDC. Important Links: https://nbcfdc.gov.in Guidelines: Scheme Guidelines File View Close

pages.title: Free Coaching
pages.text: Free Coaching Hello, world! This is a toast message. Central Scheme : Education Seed - Free Coaching Beneficiaries: DNT Students requiring coaching Eligibility: 1) Must be of DNT community 2) Must have income of less than 8 lakhs per annum 3) Students must have scored the minimum marks in the exam which is required to appear for the test. For e.g for JEE the students are required to have 75% in 12th boards. Benefits 1) Fully paid for coaching for entrance examinations upto a prescribed limit given within the SEED scheme guidelines. 2) Stipend of Rs. 1500 per month. 3) Additional support of Rs. 2000 per month for students with disabilities Important Links: https://coaching.dosje.gov.in/ Guidelines: SEED Scheme Guidlines Further Information : Clik Here PM YASASVI 1) Central Sector Scheme of Top Class Education in Schools for OBC, EBC & DNT Students Read more Beneficiaries: Students in Classes 9th entering and 11th to 12th Eligibility : Students Entering Class 10th and 12th, with Total Family Income: Less than 2.5 Lakhs, 30% Reservation for Girl Students, Students studying at top class schools as listed here. Benefit: Grants will be provided for tuition fee, hostel fee and other charges as required by the school, subject to a maximum of Rs. 75,000/- every year per student of class 9 and 10 and Rs. 1,25,000/- per annum per student of class 11 and 12. Required Documents: 1. Aadhaar Card 2. Passport size photograph 3. Educational qualification marksheets/certificates 4. Valid Income certificate issued by the competent Authority 5. Domicile Certificate 6. Caste/Community Certificate issued by the competent authority 7. Disability certificate, if applicable 8. Bank Account details Application Process: 1) Registration on the National Scholarship Portal 2) Providing details of marks recieved in the previous year's annual examination 3)Providing bank account details 4) Shortlisting of selected students and direct transfer through DBT to student's bank account Important Timelines: July to September Important Links: https://scholarships.gov.in/ Guidelines: Scheme Guidelines 2) Central Sector Scheme of Top Class Education in Colleges for OBC,EBC& DNT Students Read more Beneficiaries: College and Graduation Students Eligibility: Total Family Annual Income: Less than 2.5Lakhs, Student must be OBC, DNT or EBC to be eligible, 30% Reservation for Girl Students, Students must have gotten admission into one of the colleges listed here 1) Scholarship of Rs. 2 lakhs per annum per student for Private sector institutions 2)Living Expenses of Rs.3000, Books & Stationery of 5,000/annum 3) Latest Computer/Laptop of Rs.45,000. Required Documents: 1. Aadhaar Card 2. Passport size photograph 3. Educational qualification marksheets/certificates 4. Valid Income certificate issued by the competent Authority 5. Domicile Certificate 6. Caste/Community Certificate issued by the competent authority 7. Disability certificate, if applicable 8. Bank Account details 9. Admission Rank Proof 10. Proof of fee details to claim the scholarship Application Process: 1) Students who have been selected on the basis of merit as per various competitive exams can apply. 2) Eligible Students can apply for the scheme on the National Scholarship Portal where they will have to upload their income certificate and caste certificate, along with details of their bank account, admission rank and fee details. The institution will confirm all of these details and then forward them to the Ministry. Important Timelines: At time of admission into the institute, or at the start of the new academic year (for renewal) Important Links: https://scholarships.gov.in/ Important Guidelines: SchemeGuidelines 3) Pre Matric Scholarship Read more Beneficiaries: Students in Class 9th and 10th Eligibility: Total Family Annual Income: Less than 2.5Lakhs, Student must be OBC, DNT or EBC to be eligible, 30% Reservation for Girl Students, 5% Reservation for Persons with Disabilities Benefits: Academic Allowance of 4,000Rs. per annum Required Documents: 1. Aadhar Card 2. DNT Certificate 3. Income Certificate Application Process: Signing up on the YASASVI Website and applying on the portal Important Timelines: Submission of Application on Portal - 1st April to 30th June| Verification by the Institutions- 31st July| Verification by the States 15th August | Scholarship Amount released by 30th September Guidelines: Scheme Guidelines 4) Post Metric Scholarship Read more Beneficiaries:Students in Class 10th, Students in Class 12th, Students applying for MA, PhD, Bachelor's , Post Graduate Diplomas Eligibility: Total Family Annual Income: Less than 2.5 Lakhs, Student must be of OBC, EBC or DNT community. Benefits: | Category of courses | Academic Allowance | Tuition Fee | Total | | Group 1: Degree and Post Graduate level professional courses | 10000 | 10000 | 20000 | | Group 2: Other Professional Courses leading to Degree, Diploma, Certificate | 8000 | 5000 | 13000 | | Group 3: Graduate and Post Graduate courses not covered under Group I & Group II | 6000 | 2000 | 8000 | | Group 4: All post-matriculation (Post Class X level) non-degree courses | 5000 | 0 | 5000 | Required Documents: 1. YASASVI Portal ID 2. Aadhar Card 3. Annual Income Certificate 1. Registration by Student on the YASASVI Portal much before the start of college 2. Application for FREESHIP CARD which will be issued by the State Government, this makes the student eligible to study in the institution without pre-payment of fees 3. The scholarship amount is disbursed as the student begins their academic session Scholarships remain open throughout the year to apply for a Freeship card Portal Opens on 1st of April every year. Scholarship Payment: Before 30th September Guidelines: Scheme Guidelines Central Sector Scheme of National Overseas Scholarship Beneficiaries: Indian graduate students pursuing Masters and Ph.D courses abroad Eligibility: 1) Have Atleast 60% marks or equivalent grade for the qualifying exam, For Masters: Qualifying exam is Bachelor's. For PhD, Qualifying exam is Master's. 2) The applicant must be of age 35 or less on 1st April of the selection year 3) Total Family Income per annum must not exceed 8 lakhs per annum. 4) College/University must be ranked in top 500 as per QS World Rankings 5) Applicant must belong to OBC/SC/EWS or DNT community. 6) There are 6 seats out of 125 reserved for DNT and Semi Nomadic Tribes Benefits Support Duration: 4 years for PhD, 3 years for Masters For More information : Read more Financial Assistance: 1) Annual Maintenance Allowance: 15400USD/ 9900 British Pounds 2) Annual Contingency Allowance: 1500 Dollars 3) Total Tuition Fees 4) Total Visa Fees 5) Medical Insurance Premium 6) Incidental Journey Allowance and Equipment Allowance: 20 US dollars or Indian Rupee equivalent 7) Air Passage: Airplane tickets to the institute and back on the shortest route, of economy class are allowed to be purchased. It must be bought only from a) IRCTC b) BLCL c) Ashok Travels and Tourism Required Documents: During Application: 1. 10th Board Certificate 2. DNT Certificate 3. Photo 4. Scanned Signature 5. Current Address proof/Permanent Address Proof, in case different from current address 6. Qualifying Degree/Provisional Certificate 7. Mark sheet of qualifying examination 8. Valid Document regarding admission in Foreign University- Admission offer should be unconditional 9. Income documents of all family members 10. Employer's NOC Certificate if applicant is employed. Gap certificate in case there is gap of more than 6 months after completing qualifying degree. 11. ITR acceptance document. 12. Aadhar Card Application Process: Selection Period is April to March of next year. For selections for 2024-25, applications will be opened from 15th February 2024 to 31st March 2024. Application must happen online with all the listed documents for Round 1 on the link below. Important Timelines 1) Portal Opens from 15th February to 31st March of each year for Round 1 of selections. 2)Application will be processed in 20-30 days. 3) Selection Committee will take 15 days to evaluate the application. 4) The final list will be shared within the first week of September. 5) Round 2 Applications for the next selection year will begin in first week of September. Important Link: www.nosmsje.gov.in Guidelines: https://www.nosmsje.gov.in/nosmsje/public/images/NOS-Scheme-Guidelines-2024-25.pdf File View Close

pages.title: Login : Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNTs (SEED)
pages.text: Login : Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNTs (SEED) Hello, world! This is a toast message. Login File View Close

Hello, world! This is a toast message. FAQ's What is DWBDNC? The Development and Welfare Board for De-notified, Nomadic, and Semi-Nomadic Communities DWBDNC) was constituted on February 21, 2019, to address the unique needs of marginalized communities in India. Historically, these communities faced exclusion and marginalization, including being labeled under the Criminal Tribes Act of 1871. Following the repeal of this Act, DWBDNC continues the government's efforts towards their rehabilitation and empowerment. We focus on addressing the challenges faced by these communities. Our mission is to provide support and services, promoting social and economic empowerment, and ensuring these communities receive the support they deserve. What is the SEED scheme? The Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNTs (SEED) is a program overseen by DWBDNC to provide economic support and welfare services to De-notified, Nomadic, and Semi-Nomadic Communities. The Scheme has been formulated with four components that affect their livelihood. The four component of the SEED scheme are: To provide coaching of good quality for DNT candidates to enable them to appear in competitive examinations like Civil Services examination, Staff Selection Commission (SSC), NEET examination for pursuing MBBS, IIT-JEE, Banking examination etc.; To assist in getting health insurance (ayushman card) benefit to DNT Communities through Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PMJAY) of National Health Authority. To facilitate livelihoods initiative to support income generation at community level to build and strengthen small clusters of DNT/NT/SNT Communities institutions. Who is eligible for the SEED scheme? The eligibility criteria for the Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNTs (SEED) is primarily based on income and community status. The scheme is for DNT (De-notified Tribes), NT (Nomadic Tribes), and SNT (Semi-Nomadic Tribes) communities. Only students belonging to DNT communities with an annual family income of rupees 8 lakh or less per annum and not availing any such benefits from similar Central or State government will be eligible schemes. How is the SEED scheme implemented? The scheme is implemented by DWBDNC in collaboration with designated agencies, NGOs and other stakeholders. What information is required to process the registration on SEED? To process the registration on SEED, you will need to provide your Aadhaar card, a valid email ID, and a contact number for correspondence and communication. Please ensure that you provide accurate and valid information. File View Close

pages.title: Privacy Policy
pages.text: Privacy Policy Hello, world! This is a toast message. Privacy Policy We do not collect personal information, like names or addresses, when you visit our website. If you choose to provide that information to us, it's only used to -fulfill your request for information. We do collect some technical information when you visit to make your visit seamless. The section below explains how we handle and collect technical information when you visit our website. Information collected and stored automatically When you browse, read pages, or download information on website of D/o SJ&E, we automatically gather and store certain technical information about your visit. This information never identifies who you are. The information we collect and store about your visit is listed below: The IP address (IP address is a number that is automatically assigned to your computer whenever you are surfing the web) from which you access our website. The type of browser (such as Firefox, Netscape, or Internet Explorer) and operating system (Windows, Unix) used to access our site The date and time you accessed our site The pages/URLs you have visited and This information is only used to help us make the site more useful for you. With this data, we learn about the number of visitors to our site and the types of technology our visitors use. We never track or record information about individuals and their visits. Cookies When you visit some websites, they may download small pieces of software on your computer/browsing device known as cookies. Some cookies collect personal information to recognize your computer in the future. We only use non-persistent cookies or per- session cookies. Per-session cookies serve technical purposes, like providing seamless navigation through this website. These cookies do not collect personal information on users and they are deleted as soon as you leave our website. The cookies do not permanently record data and they are not stored on your computer’s hard drive. The cookies are stored in memory and are only available during an active browser session. Again, once you close your browser, the cookie disappears. If you send us personal information We do not collect personal information for any purpose other than to respond to you (for example, to respond to your questions or provide subscriptions you have chosen). If you choose to provide us with personal information like filling out a Contact Us form, with an e-mail address and pin code, and submitting it to us through the website we use that information to respond to your message, and to help get you the information you are requested. We only share the information you give us with another government agency if your question relates to that agency, or as otherwise required by law. Our website never collects information or creates individual profiles for commercial marketing. While you MUST provide an e-mail address for a localized response to any incoming questions or comments to us, we recommend that you do NOT include any other personal information. File View Close

6 Background of the 
Beneficiaries 
a. Challenges: Vasundra Nayak (24) and Kanta (19) are both from 
Norangdesar village in Bikaner and belong to the Nayak 
(Denotified) community, which has historically remained very poor 
and deprived of education. The community had not benefited from 
government schemes earlier. About 11 months before joining 
SEED, DCCP Mane Khan visited the village and explained the SEED 
Project. Vasundra joined to make her community self -reliant. 
Kanta, who had always been interested in computers, finally had 
an opportunity to formally learn them and take it up as a 
livelihood. 
 
b. Barriers: 
• Educational: Low formal vocational / technical training in the 
community before SEED.  
• Economic: No regular independent income; dependence on 
family / irregular wages; no savings or bank linkage.  
• Social: Social stigma faced by DNT / Naik communities; isolation 
from mainstream services.  
• Geographical: Resides in a remote village with limited access to 
markets, training centres and institutional support.  
• Documentation: Many community members lacke d PAN cards, 
Aadhaar, Ayushman cards and Ghumantu certificates.  
• Financial: No SHG / bank linkage previously; no capital to start 
any micro-enterprise. 
• Occupational: No earlier formal computer training in the 
community despite the interest.  
 
c. Aspirations / Goals: 
• To stand on their own feet and run a small enterprise as a 
community of women. 
• To set up and operate a computer training centre and cybercafé 
in the village. 
• To extend E-Mitra services to their community and earn a regular 
income. 
• To help SHG members get PAN, Aadhaar updates, Ayushman 
cards and Ghumantu certificates.  
• To upgrade the centre into an E -Mitra centre in the near future.  
• To strengthen their own and group members' financial position.  
 
d. Family / Community Status: Both Vasundra and Kanta belong to 
      SEED Scheme — Success Stories | DWBDNC | Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment | Govt. of India                         25 | P a g e  
 
Sr. Particulars Response 
Nayak / DNT households in Norangdesar. The community had been 
historically poor and outside the reach of welfare schemes and 
formal vocational training. Before SEED, neither had any 
independent earning or exposure to formal banking , enterprise or 
technical training. 
7 Help from Scheme 
Intervention & 
Current Status 
Scheme Intervention: Under the SEED Project, Vasundra, Kanta 
and 9 other women together formed the Karni Mata SHG (11 
members) with the support of CCP and DCCP. The SHG opened a 
bank account in its name and received ₹25,000 as Revolving Fund 
(RF) and ₹25,000 as Entrepreneurship Promotion Assistance (EPA). 
With the EPA amount, the group purchased computers and took 
one month of computer training; trainers were appointed an d 
proper computer training was delivered to the SHG members. The 
group also completed E-Mitra registration. 
 
Current Status: 
• Karni Mata SHG is running a Computer Training Centre and Cyber 
Café in Norangdesar. 
• Children and young girls of the village com e to learn computers 
— generating teaching income.  
• E-Mitra registration is done, enabling E -Mitra services through 
the centre. 
• Income from the centre is used for SHG savings and routine 
expenses (electricity bill etc.).  
• Vasundra has also started pers onal income-generating work 
using the RF; her income and confidence have grown.  
• The SHG has helped members get PAN cards, Ayushman cards, 
Aadhaar updates and Ghumantu (Nomadic) certificates.  
• Plans are in motion to convert the centre into a full E -Mitra 
centre, which will significantly strengthen Vasundra, Kanta and 
their group members' economic situation and open up more 
opportunities. 
• Kanta has gained a future-oriented digital skill that very few 
young women in her community have.  
• Both Vasundra and Kanta are now creating a new identity for 
themselves and their community.  
8 Type of Support Revolving Fund (RF) + EPA (Entrepreneurship Promotion 
Assistance) + one-month Computer Skill Training under the SEED 
Scheme, combined with SHG infrastructure (com puter centre / 
      SEED Scheme — Success Stories | DWBDNC | Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment | Govt. of India                         26 | P a g e  
 
Sr. Particulars Response 
cybercafé). 
9 Support / Amount 
Provided 
₹25,000 (Revolving Fund) + ₹25,000 (EPA Fund) provided to the 
Karni Mata SHG. EPA fund used to purchase computers and 
conduct one-month computer training; RF used for member -level 
income generation. Computer Training Centre / Cyber Café 
infrastructure and E-Mitra registration also made available through 
the SEED Project at the Karni Mata SHG.  
10 Change in Income Before: No independent income; community had no exposure to 
enterprise. 
After: Earning from the village Computer Training Centre / Cyber 
Café and E-Mitra services; income from the cybercafé used for 
group savings and operational expenses; further income expected 
once E-Mitra services are fully activated. Vasundra has additional 
income from RF-supported personal enterprise.  
Impact: From a deprived community with no scheme reach, to 
running the village's own computer centre, cybercafé and E -Mitra 
point — a transformational shift in livelihood and dignity for both 
Vasundra and Kanta, and a generational leap for DNT / Naik young 
women in a remote village.

5 Introduction Lobhipant Mahila Bachat Gat, located at Dhumka in Yavatmal district, is a 
women-led Self-Help Group formed with the aim of improving the livelihood 
opportunities of its members. Most of the members belong to economically 
weaker families and depended on seasonal labor work for survival.  
6 Need for 
Support 
The SHG members observed that people in nearby villages frequently required 
cooking utensils for weddings, social gatherings, religious programs, and other 
public functions. However, due to lack of financial resources, the group was 
unable to purchase the necessary utensils and start a rental service.  
7 Support 
through SEED 
Scheme 
Under the SEED Scheme implemented by the National Backward Classes 
Finance and Development Corporation (NBCFDC) and DHAN Foundation, the 
SHG received assistance under the EPA activity. With this support, the group 
purchased large cooking utensils and related materials required for community 
events and functions. 

      SEED Scheme — Success Stories | DWBDNC | Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment | Govt. of India                         55 | P a g e  
 
   
8 Implementatio
n of the Activity 
After receiving the assistance, the SHG started providing utensils on rent for 
marriage ceremonies, religious programs, village festivals, and other public and 
social functions. The activity was managed collectively by the group members, 
including booking, maintenance, and collection of rental charges.  
9 Positive 
Outcomes 
• The SHG established a stable group -based business 
• Members started earning additional regular income  
• Dependence on irregular wage labor reduced  
• Women members became more financially active and confident  
• The SHG gained social recognition in the village and nearby areas  
• The activity strengthened unity and teamwork among the members  
10 Overall Impact The utensil rental business created a sustainable livelihood opp ortunity for the 
SHG members. The support received under the SEED Scheme not only 
improved the economic condition of the group but also encouraged women’s 
participation in community-based enterprises. 
11 Conclusion The assistance provided by NBCFDC and DHAN Foundation under the SEED 
Scheme played an important role in empowering Anusaya Ulavarham Mahila 
Bachat Gat. By starting a utensil rental service, the SHG created a reliable 
source of income and improved the socio -economic condition of its members. 
This success story demonstrates how community -based livelihood activities can 
promote women’s empowerment and financial self -reliance.

6 Background of 
the Beneficiary 
Mrs. Ashwini Kanhoji Khadse is a resident of Dhangarwadi, Taluka Sengaon, 
District Hingoli. She belongs to the Dhangar community under the 
Denotified, Nomadic and Semi-Nomadic Tribes (DNTs). The women in her 
group belonged to economically weaker backgrounds and did not have 
permanent housing or stable sources of income. Earlier, they worked as 
agricultural labourers and daily wage workers in small farms and nearby 
areas. Due to low educational attainment, lack of skills, and limited 
livelihood resources in the village, they faced continuous economic 
hardships. Even for public functions, utensils and basic resources had to be 
borrowed from outside the vill age. Most families did not own agricultural 
land and depended on wage labour in others’ fields for survival. Later, they 
came to know about the SEED Scheme through DHAN Foundation, which 
provided opportunities for savings, self -employment, and financial support 
for community-based livelihood activities.  
7 Support Through 
Scheme 
Intervention & 
Present Status 
Through the SEED Project supported by the Central Government and 
implemented by DHAN Foundation, the Radha Kalanjiyam Women’s Self -
Help Group was established in Dhangarwadi in May 2025. Mrs. Ashwini 
Kanhoji Khadse became associated with the group and later opened the SHG 
account in a nationalized bank. The group members selected her as the 
Group President. Under the SEED Scheme, she received assistance t o 
purchase two goats for her family. Goat rearing improved the family’s 
livelihood and reduced their financial difficulties. Through the project, the 
women also gained access to savings practices, banking services, and 
awareness about livelihood opportunit ies. Activities such as Revolving Fund 
(RF) and Entry Point Activities (EPA) are also being implemented in the 
village. Today, the beneficiary and her family have achieved greater 
livelihood security and economic stability through the SEED Project.  
8 Type of 
Assistance 
Revolving Fund assistance under the SEED Scheme  
9 Amount / 
Assistance 
Provided 
₹25,000 (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand) 
10 Change in Income Earlier, the beneficiary faced difficulty even in contributing savings to the 
group. At present, she is able to save ₹200 every month regularly, reflecting 
improvement in the family’s financial condition and livelihood stability.  
11 Date / Year of 
Assistance 
April 2026

6 Background of the 
Beneficiary 
Mrs. Uma Netaji Karad is a resident of Malkaranja village, Taluka Kallamb, 
District Osmanabad. She owns one acre of land in a drought -prone area 
where recurring droughts affected agricultural productivity. Due to 
dependence on non-cash crops, the family’s annual income remained low 
and uncertain. Her husband worked as a bus driver, but his earnings alone 
were insufficient to support the household. To supplement the family 
income, Uma managed two cows and earned partially through milk sales; 
however, the income was still inadequate to meet growing family needs.  
7 Problems Faced 
by the Beneficiary 
Low and uncertain agricultural income due to drought conditions, limited 
livelihood opportunities, inadequate household income, dependence on 
small-scale farming and milk sales, and lack of awareness regarding 
integrated livelihood opportunities.  
8 Economic / Social 
/ Educational 
Barriers 
Economic: Small landholding, drought -affected farming, low and unstable 
income, and limited livelihood resources.Social: Limited economic 
opportunities for rural women.Livelihood: Underutilization of available 
livestock resources and infrastructure before project intervention.  
9 Goals / 
Aspirations of the 
Beneficiary 
To create a stable and sustainable source of income, expand goat rearing 
and poultry farming, improve family living standards, and strengthen 
financial independence through integrated farming practices.  
10 Family Situation The family depended on small -scale farming, the husband’s bus driving 

      SEED Scheme — Success Stories | DWBDNC | Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment | Govt. of India                         73 | P a g e  
 
income, and limited dairy earnings. The available income was insufficient to 
meet household expenses and future needs.  
11 Support Through 
Scheme 
Intervention & 
Present Status 
In January 2025, Mrs. Uma Karad joined the NSFDC –SEED Project through 
her Self-Help Group (SHG). Under the  Revolving Fund, she received a loan of 
₹10,000, which she invested in purchasing a pregnant goat for ₹7,000 and 
Desi hens worth ₹3,000. She successfully integrated dairy, goat rearing, and 
poultry farming by utilizing cow fodder for goats and reusing live stock waste 
productively, creating a sustainable zero -wastage livelihood model. Within 
seven months, the goat gave birth to three kids, increasing livestock assets 
to approximately ₹25,000. Poultry farming now generates around ₹3,000 
per month, while dairy activities continue to support household income. 
The SHG also utilized EPA funds to establish a water tank in the village, 
improving access to water and community facilities.  
12 Type of Assistance Revolving Fund loan assistance for goat rearing and poult ry farming, along 
with village infrastructure support under EPA

farming activities, along with water tank installation in the village under

the monthly household income increased by nearly ₹4,500. Livestock assets 
also increased significantly, with goat assets growing from ₹7,000 to 
approximately ₹25,000 within a few months.

Mrs. Dipali Premchand Munde is a resident of Govindpur village, Taluka 
Kalamb, District Osmanabad. Her family of six members depended mainly 
on small-scale farming and irregular daily wage labo ur for survival. Due to 
limited landholding and resources, the family struggled to meet monthly 
household expenses. Water scarcity was another major challenge in the 
village, forcing women to fetch water from distant places, which consumed 
time and reduced opportunities for income-generating work. As a result, 
the family income remained unstable and limited to subsistence living.

Navchetna Sanstha, the beneficiary became associated with a Self -Help 
Group (SHG) in January 2025. The SHG received ₹25,000 under the Entry 
Point Activity (EPA), which was utilized for installation of a village water 
tank, improving access to water for domestic use.  Under the Revolving 
Fund (RF), Dipali received a loan of ₹15,000 for goat rearing. Using this 
support, she purchased goats and started goat farming. Her family income 
improved through goat milk sales, loan repayments are being made 
regularly, and she has continued monthly savings through the SHG. Her 
confidence, awareness of banking systems, and participation in group 
activities have significantly improved.

6 Background of 
the 
Beneficiary 
Challenges: The family survived through goat rearing with limited 
resources, resulting in a weak economic condition. Barriers: Due to 
living in a rural area and being l ocated far from the city, access to 
educational, economic, social, and geographical facilities was 
limited.Aspirations / Goals: To improve the family’s condition 
through business or employment opportunities and ensure complete 
literacy for their children.Family / Community Status: The family 
belonged to an economically weaker section with limited livelihood 
resources. 
7 Support 
Through 
Scheme 
Intervention 
& Present 
Status 
With the support of RF and EPA funds, the women members of the 
group decided to invest in goat rearing, which is a low-cost, 
sustainable, and reliable source of income. The women purchased 
goats and started goat-rearing activities. This initiative not only 
strengthened their economic condition but also improved their 
decision-making ability, leadership skills, and self-confidence. The 
group members now understand the importance of savings, 
discipline, and mutual cooperation. They are planning to utilize their 
income for children’s education, household needs, and future 
investments.

Story 60 
 
Sr. 
No. 
Particulars Response 
1 Name of 
Beneficiary / 
Group 
Mehra Maa Self-Help Group (SHG) 
2 Age 10 Months (since formation of the group)  
3 Gender Women (All members are women)  
4 Category / 
Community 
Denotified, Nomadic and Semi-Nomadic Tribes (DNTs) 
5 Occupation / 
Educational 
Status 
Business – Handicraft work / handmade textile products 
(handmade bags, embroidered clothes, bedsheets, pillow 
covers)Educational Status – Studied between Class 5th to 
8thLocation – Amarwara, Amarwara Block, Chhindwara Distri ct, 
Madhya PradeshName of President – Sunita Pawar 
6 Background of 
the Beneficiary 
Challenges: The women of this group earlier worked as unskilled 
labourers in brick kilns and coal mines. Continuous exposure to 
dust, smoke, heat, and heavy labour severely  affected their 
health. Many women suffered from breathing problems and joint 
pain. Their children were also engaged in labour work at the kilns, 
which interrupted their education completely. During the 
formation of the group, the women faced resistance fr om kiln 
owners who did not want to lose cheap labour. Barriers:- 
Educational: Most women never had the opportunity to attend 
school and were unable to read or write properly. - Physical: Long-
term kiln work caused back pain, breathing difficulties, and eye 
irritation.- Economic: Daily wages were only around ₹150 –200 and 
work was irregular. During the rainy season, there was almost no 
work, leading to severe hardship.- Social: Women working at kilns 
were looked down upon socially. - Geographical: The kiln was 

 
      SEED Scheme — Success Stories | DWBDNC | Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment | Govt. of India                         
113 | P a g e  
 
located around 15 km away from the village, consuming an entire 
day in travel and work.- Business-related: Although the women 
were interested in handicrafts, they had neither training nor 
access to materials and capital. - Debt Burden: Many women had 
borrowed money from local moneylenders at very high monthly 
interest rates (15–20%) for medical treatment and household 
expenses.Aspirations / Goals:- To free themselves from 
exploitative kiln labour and work from home. - To send their 
children to school and prevent them from working in kilns. - To 
improve health conditions and reduce illnesses. - To repay debts 
taken from moneylenders.- To establish a small handicraft 
business.Family / Community Status:  All members belong to the 
DNT community and come from extremely  poor families. Many 
husbands were either unemployed or addicted to alcohol, leaving 
the burden of the household entirely on the women. Most 
families lived in tin sheds or huts covered with plastic sheets. 
Children were undernourished. Before the formation  of the group, 
many women did not even have bank accounts, Aadhaar cards, or 
ration cards. They never believed they could work outside the 
brick kilns. 
7 Support Through 
Scheme 
Intervention & 
Present Status 
Under the Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNT  
Communities (SEED Scheme), the group received Revolving Fund 
assistance of ₹25,000.With this support, the group purchased four 
handloom/handicraft machines, cloth materials, colors, threads, 
embroidery design tools, beads, zippers, buttons, lace, and othe r 
raw materials. A small room in one member’s house was 
converted into a handicraft workshop. The women also received 
15 days of handicraft and designing training. Through group 
support, all members obtained Aadhaar cards, bank accounts, and 
ration cards.Current Status:- The group now produces handmade 
shopping bags, school bags, embroidered pillow covers, 
bedsheets, and festive decorative items such as wall hangings and 
torans.- The group earns an average collective income of ₹5,000 –
6,000 per month, with each member earning an additional ₹900 –
1,100 monthly income.- All women have completely stopped 
working in brick kilns and now work safely from home. - Their 
health has improved significantly, especially respiratory 
problems.- Children have started attending  school regularly, and 
the group has collectively decided that no child will work in brick 
kilns again.- All five members now have active bank accounts and 
are saving ₹50 per member every month (total ₹250 per month). - 
Members have gradually started repayi ng debts, and one member 
has already fully repaid a loan of ₹5,000 with interest. - The 
women now regularly visit local health centers for check -ups.- 
Their identity in the community has transformed from “brick kiln 
labourers” to “skilled artisan women.”  
8 Type of Revolving Fund assistance under the SEED Scheme

6 Background of the

Challenges: The women of this group faced severe social 
exclusion and discrimination. Earlier, they survived by 
collecting scrap, cleaning drains, and doing sanitation work in 
households. They were t reated as untouchables by upper -
caste villagers. Even after forming the SHG, they were not 
allowed to hold meetings inside the village and had to meet 
under a tree outside the village. Barriers: - Severe social 
discrimination and untouchability practices - Almost all 
members were illiterate, and children often dropped out of 
school due to discrimination - Very low and irregular income 
from sanitation work and scrap collection - Settlements 
located outside the village with poor access to water, 
electricity, and roads - Lack of market access despite having 
traditional handicraft skills - Low self -confidence and deep 
social insecurity Aspirations/Goals: - To live a life of dignity 
and social acceptance - To earn through handmade products 
and become self-reliant - To educate their children and protect 
them from discrimination - To prove that they are capable of 
building successful livelihoods Family/Community Status:  
Families lived in temporary huts outside the village with 
minimal facilities. Most members lacked  bank accounts, 
identity documents, and awareness of government schemes.  
7 Assistance through 
Scheme & Present 
Status

communities, the group received a Revolving Fund (RF) 
support of ₹25,000. With this assistance, the group purchased 
handicraft tool kits, beads, threads, decorative materials, and 
embroidery equipment. They also underwent a 15 -day 
specialized handicraft training program. Bank accounts, 
Aadhaar cards, and ration cards were arranged for all 
members. Present Status:  - The group now produces

- Their products are now sold in local fairs and women’s 
markets. - Social discrimination has reduced significantly, and

Members save ₹100 per month each, creating a collective 
monthly saving of ₹1,000. - The group has become an 
inspiration for other DNT communities facing social exclusion.

6 Background of the 
Beneficiary 
Challenges: The women of this group previously survived by 
collecting old clothes, plastic, glass pieces, and scrap from 
households. They wer e often insulted and called “kabadi” 
(scrap collectors) and treated disrespectfully by society. 
Their income was extremely low and irregular, and they 
often faced humiliation while working. Even their husbands 
initially opposed the idea of starting a busin ess, doubting 
that women could manufacture cleaning products. Barriers: 
- Social discrimination and lack of dignity in livelihood - Daily 
income of only ₹50 –100 through irregular scrap collection - 
Most members were illiterate and had never attended 
school - Strong gender -based resistance from family 
members - Remote location with poor road connectivity 
and long distance from the city - No prior knowledge of 
chemical-based product manufacturing - Low self -
confidence and fear of failure Aspirations/Goals: - To leave 
scrap collection work and gain social respect - To prove 
their abilities and become financially independent - To earn 
a stable and regular income - To educate their children and 
provide them with a better future - To proudly sell products 
made by their own hands Family/Community Status:  All 10 
women belong to the DNT community. Their families lived 
in extreme poverty, and both women and children were 
engaged in scrap collection or daily wage labour. Most 
members did not even have bank accounts befo re joining 
the SHG. 
7 Assistance through 
Scheme & Present 
Status 
Under the SEED Scheme for economic empowerment of 
DNT communities, the group received a Revolving Fund (RF) 
assistance of ₹25,000. The women underwent a 15 -day 
specialized training program on manufacturing cleaning 
products. With this support, the group pu rchased 
chemicals, fragrance oils, colours, packaging materials, 
bottles, pouches, mixing buckets, and measuring tools. A 
small room in the village was converted into a workshop. 
Initially, they prepared sample handwash and dishwash 
products and distribute d them free of cost to collect 
feedback. Present Status: - The group now manufactures six 
types of cleaning products including handwash, dishwash 
liquid, surf active powder, liquid soap, and fabric softener. - 
The group earns an average collective monthly income of 
₹14,000–₹16,000. - Each woman now earns an additional 
₹1,400–₹1,600 per month through organized home -based 
work. - All 10 members save ₹100 per month each, creating 
 
      SEED Scheme — Success Stories | DWBDNC | Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment | Govt. of India

a collective monthly saving of ₹1,000, with more than 
₹9,000 already saved. - Their products are now sold through 
local grocery shops, schools, and home delivery services, 
with more than 300 units sold in a month. - Husbands who 
once opposed the initiative are now actively supporting 
transportation and product delivery. - The women now  
proudly identify themselves as “entrepreneurs” instead of 
scrap collectors.

Family/Community Status: All 10 women belong to the DNT

7 Assistance through

Under the SEED Scheme for economic empowerment of 
DNT communities, the group received a Revolving Fund (RF) 
assistance of ₹25,000. The members received a 15 -day 
specialized food processing training covering FSSAI 
standards, hygiene practices, packaging, labelling, shelf -life 
management, and marketing techniques. The group was 
also supported in obtaining FSSAI registration. With the 
assistance, the group purchased raw materials such as 
mangoes, tomatoes, spices, sug ar, salt, oil, vinegar, and 
preservatives. They also procured jars, bottles, packaging 
boxes, label stickers, sealing machines, steel utensils, 
mixers, weighing scales, and other food processing 
equipment. A clean village-based workshop was established 
to maintain hygiene standards. Present Status: - The group 
now produces seven types of food products including

a. Challenges: The women of this group previously worked in

equipment. They earned around ₹100 –200 per day, but work 
was available only for 10–15 days a month. During group 
formation, the women feared that if they left this work, t hey 
would not find any alternative source of livelihood. b. 
Barriers: - Physical: Constant exposure to dirty water caused 
skin infections, respiratory illnesses, and eye irritation. Some 
women suffered from tuberculosis and asthma. - Economic: 
Monthly income was only ₹1,000–1,500 with no savings. - 
Educational: Most members had studied only up to Classes 5 –
8, while some were illiterate. - Social: Due to their sanitation 
work, they faced discrimination and were often called “dirty” 
by society. - Professional: They had no prior experience in 
manufacturing cleaning products. c. Aspirations/Goals: - To 
leave unsafe and unhygienic jobs. - To establish a clean and 
respectable business. - To improve their health and living 
conditions. - To ensure good education for  their children so 
they would not have to do similar work. - To create a stable 
and regular source of income. d. Family/Community 
Situation: All 10 women belong to the DNT community. Their 
husbands work as daily wage labourers or remain 
unemployed. Most families have 2–3 children, many of whom

hardship. None of the women had bank accounts earlier, and 
most families lived in temporary huts. During group 
formation, the women encouraged one another to mov e away 
from unsafe and degrading work.

the Scheme & 
Present Status

economic empowerment of the DNT community, the group

15 days of intensive training in cleaning product 
manufacturing, including safe chemical handling, quality 
control, packaging, and marketing techniques. With this 
support, the group: - Purchased chemicals such as caustic 
soda, laboline, fragrance oils, colou rs, and sodium 
hypochlorite. - Purchased 100 large bottles (5 litres), 300 small 
bottles (200ml and 500ml), 500 small pouches, label stickers, 
and packaging materials. - Purchased buckets, mixing rods,

6 Background of the

a. Challenges: The women of this group were extremely 
poor and had no independent source of income. Their 
families depended entirely on the irregular daily wages 
earned by their husbands (₹200 –250 per day, and only for 
10–15 days a month). Managing household expenses was 
very difficult. Although the women were highly skilled in 
preparing pickles, papad, sauces, and other food items, 
they had never imagined these products could become a 
source of income. At the time of group fo rmation, they 
feared whether people would actually buy the food 
products they prepared. b. Barriers: - Economic: Average 
monthly family income was only ₹2,000 –3,000 for families 
of 5–6 members, reflecting extreme poverty. - Educational: 
Most women had studied between Class 5th and 8th, while 
some were illiterate. - Social: Women stepping out to start 
a business was not considered acceptable in the village, 
and many husbands and elders opposed the idea. - 
Geographical: Jamanya village is located in a remote area, 
with the nearest market 25 km away and poor road 
connectivity, especially during the rainy season. - 
Professional: While they possessed cooking skills, they 
lacked knowledge about packaging, labeling, preservatives, 
shelf life, and FSSAI standards. - Mental: A belief prevailed 
that “village women cannot run businesses.” c. 
Aspirations/Goals: - To transform their cooking skills into a 
business and increase family income. - To provide quality 
education to their children. - To become self-reliant and 
reduce the financial burden on their families. - To obtain 
FSSAI certification and produce standardized products. - To 
inspire other women in the village. d. Family/Community 
Situation: All 10 women belong to the DNT community and 
live in Jamanya village, located 20 km away from the block 
headquarters. Their husbands work as daily wage laborers, 
agricultural workers, or remain unemployed. Most families 
have 2–3 children and lived in severe poverty, sometimes 
struggling even for food. 
7 Assistance through the 
Scheme and Current 
Status 
Scheme Intervention: Under the SEED Scheme for 
economic empowerment of the DNT community, the group 
received a Revolving Fund of ₹25,000. The group also 
underwent a 15-day specialized food processing training 
program covering FSSAI standards, hygiene, packaging, 
labeling, shelf-life enhancement, and marketing 
techniques. With this support, the group purchased: - Raw 
materials such as mangoes, lemons, tomatoes, spices, 
sugar, salt, oil, vinegar, preservatives, urad dal, potatoes, 
and gooseberries - 200 glass jars (200 gm and 500 gm), 500

plastic jars, 150 bottles, label stickers, and packaging boxes

weighing machines, sealing machines, and thermometers - 
A clean room in the village to set up a hygienic workshop 
Current Status: - The group now produces 7 varieties of 
food products, including mango pickle, lemon pickle,

mixed fruit jam, gooseberry murabba, amchur powd er, and

to decide on a livelihood activity. Eventually, they decide d to start a 
home-based food products business. Barriers:- Economic: Earlier, the 
group had no monthly income or savings. - Educational: Most members 
studied only up to Classes 5 –8 and had limited accounting knowledge. - 
Social: Some people in the village did  not support women running 
businesses, and initially, many husbands opposed the idea. - 
Geographical: Jamanya village is located in a remote area, and raw 
materials had to be purchased from a town located about 25 km 
away.- Business-related: Although the women knew how to prepare 
food products, they lacked knowledge about packaging, labeling, shelf 
life, and FSSAI standards.- Time Management: Managing household 
responsibilities along with business operations was 
challenging.Aspirations / Goals:- To establish a large-scale enterprise.- 
To double the family income.- To provide quality education to their 
children.- To obtain FSSAI certification and produce standard -quality 
products.- To become self-reliant.Family / Community Status: All 10 
women belong to the DNT community and live in Jamanya village. 
Their husbands worked as daily wage labourers, farm workers, or small 
traders. Most families had 2–3 children and faced poverty. During the 
formation of the group, the women encouraged and supported each 
other to move forward collectively.  
7 Support 
Through 
Scheme 
Intervention 
& Present 
Status 
Under the Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNT Communities 
(SEED Scheme), the group received Revolving Fund assistance of 
₹25,000. The members underwent a 15 -day training program on food 
processing and business management. Support was also provided for 
MSME and FSSAI registration.With this assistance, the group 
purchased raw materials such as mangoes, lemons, tomatoes, spices, 
sugar, salt, oil, vinegar, urad dal, gram flour, and amla. They also 
purchased 200 glass jars, 400 plastic pouches, 100 bottles, label 
 
      SEED Scheme — Success Stories | DWBDNC | Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment | Govt. of India                         
134 | P a g e  
 
stickers, packaging boxes, steel utensils, cutting boards, knives, mixers, 
weighing scales, sealing machines, and thermometers. A large room in 
the village was rented for production activities, and MSME/FSSAI 
registrations were completed.Current Status:- The group now 
produces six varieties of food products, including mango pickle, mixed 
pickle, tomato sauce, papad (urad, potato, mixed), mixed fruit jam, 
and amla murabba.- The group earns an average collective income of 
₹14,000–17,000 per month, providing each member with an additional 
income of ₹1,400–1,700 per month.- All 10 members save ₹100 per 
month each, resulting in collective savings of more than ₹9,000 so far. - 
After receiving MSME and FSSAI licenses, their food products are now 
being sold in nearby town markets and schools. - The group has 
established its own brand name, “Chaupal Foods.” - Children of the 
members are now attending school regularly, and some women hav e 
started sending their children to private schools. - Husbands are now 
supporting the business by helping in procurement of raw materials 
and product delivery.- One member stated: “We are now able to save 
₹2,000–3,000 every month on our own. We never belie ved that we 
could achieve this much.”- The women are now recognized in the 
community as “progressive women entrepreneurs.”  
8 Type of 
Assistance 
Revolving Fund assistance under the SEED Scheme  
9 Amount / 
Assistance 
Provided 
₹25,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand) 
10 Change in 
Income 
Before: ₹0 per month.After: ₹1,400–1,700 additional monthly income 
per woman through organized food product business activities.The 
group’s overall monthly profit increased by approximately 600 –700%. 
Additional benefits include MSME/FSSAI licensing, brand development, 
regular schooling for children, increased social respect, and enhanced 
self-confidence among women members.  
11 Date / Year 
of Assistance 
January 2026

6 Background In Ramayan Thanda, Chittoor District, ten women from Yanadi 
families were identified and organized into a self -help group 
under the guidance of Parivartana Seva Sangh, with support from 
the DNT Board, NSFDC, and ni -msme. After a detailed survey and 
documentation process, the group was formally established as  
the Vinayaka DNT SHG. 
7 Livelihood 
Activities 
The members are engaged in tailoring and other small -scale 
livelihood activities to support their families. They conduct 
regular meetings, maintain savings, and work collectively to 
strengthen their economic independence. 
8 Financial Support 
& Impact 
This month, the SHG received a total of ₹50,000 under the 
Revolving Fund and Entry Point Activity Fund. Out of the ₹25,000 
Revolving Fund, ₹15,000 was provided as a loan to member K. 
Bhanu to start a home-based tailoring shop. With prior tailoring 
skills, she purchased sewing machines and immediately started 
her business. This financial assistance has empowered the 
members to improve their livelihoods and support their families.  
9 Acknowledgement The group expressed heartfelt gratitude to the DNT Board and 
NSFDC for their guidance and continued support

Contents
Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNT’s 
1
Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNT’s 
1. Background and Introduction
1.1	 The	 De-notified,	Nomadic	and	Semi-Nomadic	Tribes	are	the	most	neglected,	
marginalized	and	economically	and	socially	deprived	communities.	Most	of	
them	have	been	living	a	life	of	destitution	for	generations;	and	still	continue	
to	 do	 so	 with	 an	 uncertain	 and	 gloomy	 future.	 De-notified,	 Nomadic	 and	
Semi-	nomadic	Tribes	somehow	escaped	the	attention	of	our	developmental	
framework	and	thus	are	deprived	of	the	support	unlike	Scheduled	Castes	
and	Scheduled	Tribes.
1.2	 Historically,	 these	communities	never	had	access	to	private	land	or	home	
ownership.	These	tribes	used	forests	and	grazing	lands	for	their	livelihood	
and	residential	use	and	had	“strong	ecological	connections.	Many	of	them	
are	 dependent	 upon	 various	 types	 of	 natural	 resources	 and	 carve	 out	
intricate	 ecological	 niches	 for	 their	 survival.	The	 changes	 in	 ecology	 and	
environment	seriously	affect	their	livelihood	options.
1.3	 The	 de-notified	 tribes/communities	 have	 been	 wrongly	 stigmatized	 as	
crime	prone	and	subjected	to	high	handed	treatment	as	well	as	exploitation	
by	the	representatives	of	law	and	order	as	well	as	by	the	general	society.	
Some	 of	 them	 are	included	 in	 the	 list	 of	 Scheduled	 Tribes	and	 others	 are	
in	 the	 list	 of	 Scheduled	 Castes	 and	 list	 of	 backward	 classes.	 The	 special	
approach	 to	 their	 development	 has	 been	 delineated	 and	 emphasized	 in	
the	 various	 Government	 Commissions	 like	 Renke	 Commission	 2008	 and	
IdateCommission2018.
1.4	 It	 has	been	seen	that	a	large	number	of	DNTs	have	been	included	either	in	
the	list	of	SCs,	STs	or	the	OBCs	from	time	to	time.	However ,	these	DNTs	have	
not	been	able	to	take	the	benefit	of	various	developmental	schemes	being	
implemented	 for	 SCs,	 STs	and	 OBCs.	 In	 order	 to	 enable	 the	 DNTs	to	 take	
the	benefit	of	various	developmental	schemes	being	implemented	for	the	
poor ,	it	is	necessary	that	the	additional	provisions	are	made	under	existing	
welfare	schemes	for	them	as	a	separate	target	group	irrespective	of	the	fact	
whether	they	belong	to	SCs,	STs	or	OBCs.
1.5	 These	 communities	with	varying	culture	and	life	style	are	spread	all	over	
India	 and	 are	 mostly	 invisible,	 thinly	 spread	 and	 many	 migrating.	 The	
standard	 village,	 block,	 district	 developmental	 model	 mostly	 does	 not	 fit	
Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNT’s 
2
on	them.	DNT ,	NT	and	SNT	communities	are	found	mostly	in	small	clusters	
with	 limited	 capacity	 and	 skills.	 Therefore,	 any	 developmental	 approach	
for	them	have	a	strong	need	of	special	efforts	for	these	communities	in	the	
existing	welfare	programmes	of	the	Government.
1.6	 The	 various	 recommendations	 in	 the	 reports	 on	 the	 welfare	 of	 DNT	
Communities	need	very	specific	attention	in	the	context	of	state	locations	
specific	socio-	economic,	cultural,	variety	of	the	De-notified,	Nomadic	and	
semi	 Nomadic	 tribes	 to	 strengthen	 the	 programmes	 for	 the	 economic	
development,	 educational	 development,	 generation	 of	 employment	
opportunities,	social	liberation	and	full	rehabilitation	of	de-notified	tribes.
1.7	 In	 view	of	the	above	back	drop	an	Umbrella	Scheme	for	empowerment	of	DNT	
communities	have	been	prepared	and	will	have	following	four	components	
with	an	approximate	cost	of	Rs200crore	to	be	spent	over	a	period	of	5	years	
starting	FinancialYear2021-22:
	 i.	 Educational	 Empowerment	(through	government	and	non	government	
agencies)
	 ii.	 Health	(through	PMJAY	of	NHA)
	 iii.	 Livelihoods	 (through	 NRLM,	 SRLMs,	 other	 government	 and	 non	
government	agencies)
	 iv.	 Land	 and	 housing	 (through	 PMAY -G,	PMAY -U,	other	 government	
schemes)
2. Objectives of the Scheme
The	objectives	of	the	Scheme	are	as	follows.
i.	 To	 provide	coaching	of	good	quality	for	DNT	candidates	to	enable	them	to	
appear	in	competitive	examinations;
ii.	 To	provide	health	insurance	to	DNT	Communities
iii.	 To	 facilitate	livelihoods	initiative	at	community	level	to	build	and	strengthen	
small	clusters	of	DNT/NT/SNT	Communities	institutions.
iv.	 To	 provide	financial	 assistance	 for	construction	 of	 houses	 to	 members	 of	
the	DNT	Communities
3
Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNT’s 
3. Target Group& Implementation of the Scheme
The	 DNT	 Communities	 having	 total	 family	 income	 from	 all	 sources	 of	 Rs.2.50	
lakh	or	less	per	annum	and	not	availing	any	such	benefits	from	similar	Scheme	of	
Centre	Government	or	the	State	Government	will	be	eligible	for	benefits	under	the	
Scheme.
(Note	 :	 This	 income	 limit	 will	 be	 subject	 to	 regular	 changes	 as	 per	 policies	 of	
central	government	on	income	eligibility	for	various	schemes.	This	income	limit	
is	also	a	general	rule	on	income	limit,	but	there	can	be	component	wise	alteration	
in	income	limits,	to	align	that	component	with	other	central	government	schemes	
on	the	subject).
The	 DWBDNC	will	 overall	coordinate	with	 all	 government	and	 non	 government	
agencies	for	its	implementation.	The	implementation	of	the	scheme	will	not	only	
be	 done	 through	established	 govt	implementation	 system	but	 also	 through	non	
government	agencies	to	be	engaged	after	approval	of	competent	authority	from	
time	to	time	by	following	the	procedure.
Contractual/outsource	 personnel	 such	 as	 Young	Professionals,	 Consultants	 and	
Sr .	Consultants	 would	 be	 required	 for	 monitoring	 and	 co-ordination	 activities	
at	 various	 levels.	 Expenditure	 will	 be	 met	 from	 the	 admissible	 administrative	
expenses	of	the	scheme	which	would	be	around	1%	of	the	total	financial	outlay	of	
the	scheme.
4. Educational Empowerment
4.1	 Education	 is	 the	 most	 important	 lever	 for	 social,	 economic	 and	 political	
transformation.	Education	is	a	potent	tool	for	socioeconomic	mobility	and	a	
key	instrument	for	building	an	equitable	and	just	society.	It	provides	skills	
and	 competencies	 for	 economic	 wellbeing.	 A	 well-educated	 population,	
equipped	with	the	relevant	knowledge,	attitudes	and	skills	is	essential	for	
economic	and	social	development	in	the	twenty-first	century.	Accordingly,	
component	of	free	Coaching	for	DNT	Students	has	been	envisioned	for	the	
educational	empowerment	of	these	communities:
Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNT’s 
4
4.2 Free Coaching for DNT Students
4.2.1 Objective
	 The	 objective	 of	 this	 component	 is	 to	 provide	 good	 coaching	 quality	 for	
DNT	candidates	to	enable	them	to	appear	in	competitive	examinations	and	
succeed	in	obtaining	an	appropriate	job	in	Public/Private	Sector .
4.2.2 Courses for Coaching
	 The	courses	for	which	the	coaching	will	be	imparted,	will	include	below:
	 i.	 Group	 A	 and	 B	 examinations	conducted	 by	 the	 Union	 Public	 Service	
Commission	 (UPSC),	 the	 Staff	 Selection	 Commission	 (SSC)	 and	 the	
various	Railway	Recruitment	Boards	(RRBs);
	 ii.	 Group	 A	 and	 B	 examinations	 conducted	 by	 the	 State	 Public	 Service	
Commission’s;
	 iii.	 Officers’	 Grade	examinations	conducted	by	Banks,	Insurance	Companies	
and	Public	Sector	Undertakings(PSUs);
	 iv.	 Premier	 Entrance	 Examinations	 for	 admission	 in	 (a)	 Engineering	
(eg.	 IIT -JEE),	(b)	 Medical	 (eg.	 NEET),	 (c)	 Professional	 courses	 like	
Management	 (eg.	 CAT)	and	 Law	 (eg.	 CLAT),	and	 (d)	 Any	 other	 such	
disciplines	as	Ministry	may	decide	from	time	to	time.
	 v.	 Eligibility	tests/examinations	like	SAT ,	GRE,	GMAT	and	TOEFL.
	 vi.	 Entrance	 examination	tests	for	CPL	courses/	National	Defense	Academy	
and	Combined	Defense	Services
	 vii.	 Any	 other	 professional,	 occupational,	 vocational	 training	 course	
leading	 to	 self	 employment	 or	 private/govt	job	 or	 entrepreneurship	
subject	to	that	rates	of	fees/stipend/allowance	and	duration	will	not	
exceed	the	limits	given	in	para-4.2.6	and	chart	below.
4.2.3 Distribution of slots amongst types of competitive examinations :
	 Depending	 upon	 the	 demand	 and	 response	 of	 students	 from	 target	
population,	 available	 budget,	 etc	 the	 numbers	 of	 coaching	 courses	 for	
each	category	will	be	fixed	from	time	to	time,	by	competent	authorities	in	
DWBDNC.

Scheme for Economic Empowerment of DNT’s 
4.2.4 Eligibility criteria for students
	 i.	 Only	 students	 belonging	 to	 DNT	 communities	 having	 total	 family	
income	 from	 all	 sources	 of	 Rs	 8	 lakhs	 or	 less	 per	 annum	 and	 not	
availing	any	such	benefits	from	similar	Scheme	of	Centre	Government	
or	the	State	Government	will	be	eligible	for	benefits	under	the	Scheme.	
Income	 declaration	 of	 self	 employed	 parents/	 guardian	 should	 be	
in	 the	 form	 of	 a	 certificate	 issued	 by	 Revenue	Officer	 not	 below	 the	
rank	of	Tehsildar .	Employed	parents/guardians	are	required	to	obtain	
income	certificate	from	their	employer .	Employed	parents/	guardians	
should	 obtain	 consolidated	 certificate	 from	 Revenue	 Officer	 for	 any	
other	additional	source	of	income.
	 ii.	 The	 students	must	have	secured	the	minimum	marks	in	the	qualifying	
examination	of	the	competitive	exam	for	which	coaching	is	sought,	as	
prescribed	in	the	guidelines	of	the	said	competitive	exam.	However ,	if	
the	student	has	still	not	appeared	for	the	said	qualifying	examination,	
or	if	the	student	has	appeared	and	the	result	has	not	been	declared,	the	
marks	obtained	in	the	Board/Degree	exam	just	prior	to	the	qualifying	
exam	shall	be	taken	into	account.	If	the	number	of	eligible	applications	
received	is	more	than	the	given	slots,	the	selection	will	be	done	in	the	
decreasing	order	of	the	marks	obtained	in	the	qualifying	exams.
	 iii.	 For	 the	competitive	exams	for	which	the	qualifying	exam	is	class	12th,	
benefits	under	the	scheme	will	be	available	to	a	candidate	only	if	the	
candidate	has	passed	class	12th	or	is	studying	in	class	12th	as	on	the	
date	 of	 receiving	 the	 benefit	 under	 the	 scheme.	 Further ,	in	 case	 of	
competitive	exams	for	which	the	qualifying	exam	is	at	bachelor	level,	
only	students/candidates	having	completed	the	bachelor	level	course	
or	studying	in	the	final	year	of	the	bachelor	degree	course	at	the	time	
of	receiving	the	benefits	under	the	scheme	shall	be	eligible.
	 iv.	 For	 such	examinations	which	are	conducted	in	two	parts,	Preliminary	
and	Mains,	priority	in	the	selection	will	be	given	to	those	students	who	
have	at	least	once	qualified	the	preliminary	examination
	 v.	 Benefits	 under	the	Scheme	can	be	availed	by	a	particular	student	not	
more	than	twice,	irrespective	of	the	number	of	chances	he/she	may	be	
entitled	to	take	in	a	particular	competitive	examination.	The	student	
will	be	required	to	submit	an	affidavit	that	he/she	has	not	taken	benefit	
more	than	twice	under	the	Scheme.

	 vi.	 The	 candidate	shall	be	precluded	from	availing	any	other	Scholarship	
benefits	 of	 similar	 scheme	 of	 the	 Centre	 or	 State	 Governments.	 The	
candidate	will	be	required	to	upload	an	affidavit	indicating	that	he/she	
is	not	availing	scholarship	from	any	other	similar	scheme	of	the	Centre/
State.	A	list	of	beneficiaries	availing	the	scheme	will	also	be	uploaded	
on	 the	 website	of	 the	 Ministry	 of	 Social	 Justice	 &Empowerment	and	
shall	 be	 shared	with	 States/UTs	to	 avoid	possibility	 of	 simultaneous

	 vii.	 Where	 the	 competitive	 examination	 is	 conducted	 in	 two	 stages	 viz.	
Preliminary	and	Mains,	the	candidates	will	be	entitled	for	free	coaching	
for	both	the	examinations.	They	will	be	entitled	for	free	coaching	upto

4.2.5 Mode of implementation
	 The	 DWBDNC	 will	 select	 as	 many	 numbers	 of	 students	 each	 year ,	as	
available	budget	permits	and	as	per	demand	and	response	from	students	
satisfying	 eligibility	 criteria	 stated	 in	 the	 scheme.	 For	 all	 this	 procedure	
including	publicity,	mobilizing,	shortlisting	of	students	and	other	associated	
work	stated	in	Para	4	hereinbefore,	DWBDNC	will	engage	govt	or	non	govt	
agencies.	These	students	shall	have	the	liberty	to	pursue	the	coaching	course	
in	any	institute	of	their	choice.
 a) Number of candidates to be selected:
	 	 As	 stated	 above,	there	will	 be	 no	 inter-se	 division	between	 both	 the	
courses.	It	can	be	changed/fixed	by	competent	authorities	in	DWBDNC	
depending	upon	changing	requirement,	changing	utilization,	demand	
and	response	from	students
 b) Process of application:
	 	 i.	 The	 scheme	 will	 be	 widely	advertised	through	electronic	media	
etc.	 giving	 summarized	 information	 about	 the	 Scheme.	 The	
applicants	shall,	after	assessing	their	eligibility	and	suitability	as	
per	 the	 eligibility	 conditions	 of	 the	 Scheme,	 will	 be	 required	to	
apply	online	on	the	portal	of	govt	or	non	govt	agency	engaged	for	
the	purpose	along	with	details	of	course	he/she	intends	to	join.

	 	 ii.	 The	 applicants	 will	 be	 required	 to	 upload	 their	 applications	
alongwith	prescribed	supporting	documents.
	 	 iii.	 No	 offline	applications	will	be	entertained.	Only	online	applications	
complete	in	all	respects,	shall	be	considered	for	assistance.	All	the	
incomplete	applications	will	be	summarily	rejected.	However ,	the	
rejected	candidates	will	have	the	option	to	apply	in	the	subsequent	
phases	of	selection.
 c) Selection of candidates:
	 	 i.	 On	 closure	of	the	application	portal,	separate	merit	lists	shall	be	
drawn	up	of	eligible	candidates	for	both	the	levels	of	competitive	
exam	 mentioned	 in	 para4.2.3	 of	 the	 Scheme,	 by	 the	 IT	 system	
itself,	based	on	information	submitted	by	the	candidates.
	 	 ii.	 The	 applications	 will	 be	 scrutinized	 in	 the	 order	 of	 merit	 for	
ascertaining	eligibility.	Those	applications	not	found	complete,	or	
if	the	applicants	are	found	ineligible,	will	be	rejected.
	 	 iii.	 The	 order	 of	 merit	 will	 be	 as	 detailed	 in	 para4.2.4	 (ii)	 of	 the	
Scheme.
	 	 iv.	 The	 merit	 lists	 so	 finalized	 will	 be	 placed	 before	 a	 Screening	
Committee	set	up	by	the	DWBDNC,	for	making	recommendation	
about	 selection	 of	 the	 candidates.	 The	 assigned	 number	 of	
candidates	 in	 the	 merit	 will	 be	 given	the	 benefit	 of	 the	 scheme	
subject	to	verification	of	their	credentials.	The	merit	list	shall	be	
uploaded	on	the	website	of	the	Ministry.
 d) Release of assistance to the students
	 	 i.	 The	 course	fee	shall	be	released	directly	into	the	account	of	the	
beneficiary	 candidate	 in	 two	 installments	 through	 DBT	 mode.	
However ,	students	will	have	to	submit	their	bank	account	linked	
with	their	adhaar .
	 	 ii.	 After	 the	selection	and	verification	of	credentials	of	the	candidates	
an	“Open	Assurance”	letter	shall	be	issued	to	candidates	stating	
therein	 the	 name	 of	 candidate	 selected,	 rank	 no,	 details	 of	 fee	
amount	 to	 be	 given	 under	 the	 scheme.	 This	 will	 be	 used	 by	
Candidates	to	contact	coaching	institute	in	which	he/	she	wants	to

attend	coaching.	On	receipt	of	admission	confirmation/assurance

	 	 iii.	 The	 candidate	will	be	required	to	join	the	selected	course	within

4.2.6 Extent of Assistance
 i. Fees :	The	actual	 course	fee	of	the	institute	in	which	the	students	is	
applying,	 which	 the	 institute	 charges	 course	 from	 general	 students,	
or	the	prescribed	course	fee	by	the	Ministry	as	per	Annexure	of	the	
Scheme,	whichever	is	less,	subject	to	the	limits	for	fees	and	duration	of	
the	course	laid	down	in	the	Annexure.	If	the	course	fee	is	more	than	
the	admissible	amount,	the	candidate	has	to	arrange	for	the	remaining	
funds	from	his/her	own	sources.
 ii. Stipend:	 Monthly	stipend	 of	 Rs.	 1500/-	 per	 student	 will	 be	 paid	 for	
local	students	for	attending	the	coaching	class.	Similarly,	Rs.4000/-	per	
student	 will	 be	 paid	 per	 month	 for	 outstation	 students.	 The	 stipend	
will	be	paid	till	the	duration	of	the	course	or	for	one	year ,	whichever	is	
less.	The	stipend	to	each	student	shall	be	paid	directly	by	the	Ministry	
through	DBT .
